---
title: "DCA Flight Delay Classification" # TODO: better title
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-links: 
      - text: GitHub Repo
        icon: github
        target: _blank
        href: https://github.com/GW-ML-Flight/Flight-Delay
# jupyter: python3
toc: true
number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
author:
    - name: Max Eichholz
      email: m.eichholz@gwu.edu
    - name: Liza Mozolyuk
      email: lizamozolyuk@gwu.edu
    - name: Zack Rahbar
      email: zackrahbar@gwu.edu
    - name: Lauren Schmidt
      email: laurenschmidt@gwu.edu
    - name: Ozzy Simpson
      email: ozzy@gwu.edu
abstract: |
    We will write an abstract.
---

# Deliverables

> What are concrete artifacts created as the result of this project? Artifacts that existed before the project do not count (e.g., existing dataset or code base); but if you made meaningful edits to existing dataset or code base, you should list them here. For example:
>
> -   Code base implementing a neural network architecture for some task. If based on some existing code base, provide a one-sentence summary for what you changed / improved.
> -   Trained model weights that achieve x% accuracy on some task. This would have to be a model you trained.
> -   Processed and cleaned dataset for training the model. If this is based on some existing dataset, say what you changed.
> -   Leaderboard submission to some Kaggle competition that is ranked x% by date of submission (attach screenshot)
> -   Simulation environment you built to evaluate some model. If this is based on existing simulator, what did you change?
> -   Paper draft in preparation for conference / workshop submission (attach draft).
>
> You don’t have to cover all these items, nor should you limit to these.

-   Codebase – link to it

-   Trained model weights

-   Our cleaned dataset of both flight data and weather data

    -   For weather, combined ISD lite and some full data

# Experiment Design

> The main goal of this section is to understand: how did you convert the high level goal (e.g., “solve self driving”) into empirical experiments with measurable outcomes (e.g., “achieve x% score in this driving simulator?”)?
>
> If your experiment design changed a few times during the course of the project, it’s recommended that you discuss those changes and their rationale here. What are the most important decisions (e.g., “we focused on ”) in this process to ensure that the measurable outcomes best reflect the end goal you care about? What empirical results informed these decisions?
>
> In this section, it’s important that you clearly define how you measure success, and exactly how you do the evaluation.

-   We wanted to model the Wang paper [@wangCalibratedExplainableFlight2026] but with DCA as the focus. Goal was to get similar results as they did with BOS for DCA. Looking for things like ROC-AUC, F1-score, and accuracy.
-   We also wanted to try to improve upon that model with additional features. This included more weather data, more derived data, and more historical data.
-   We also tried to do more of a regression approach to predict flight delay amounts, but that approach was less successful than a classification approach. Predicting actual delay times was more challenging.

# Methodology

> If your project involves modeling (I assume most projects do), describe your modeling approach here, including but not limited to architecture, data processing. If you went through iterations of modeling throughout your project, it’s recommended that you document those in this section, in particular why you decided to make those changes.\

-   Describe our modeling approach (e.g., various classifiers, hyperparameter search, etc.).
-   Talk about our initial attempt of just doing a regression approach, that being difficult, and then going to the classification approach based on @wangCalibratedExplainableFlight2026.
-   Talk about features that we considered and ultimately did or did not include, and why: 
    - Measuring distance categorically ("short", "medium", "long", "very_long"), ultimately did not include because it did not significantly improve performance. 
    - Distance transformations including taking the log of distance and interaction (distance * weather severity), also did not include because did not significantly improve performance.

# Results

> What are the key results of your project? This should include not only the final (best) numbers you achieved in evaluation, but also how that changed over the course of the project (if you have been tracking those numbers). You can call back to the experiment design discussions about how the overall goal is converted to measurable metrics, and a discussion of what uncertainties we should maintain when interpreting these results is recommended.

-   Include our results from the notebook(s).

# Lessons Learned

> If you think there are high level lessons (not specific things like one key trick in training process), share them here. This can be technical or operational, e.g., ideal frequency and format of group meetings in a collaborative project.

-   Homebrew is quite difficult to work with, especially on Apple Silicon. We spent a good amount of time troubleshooting XGBoost issues incorrectly stating we were running 32-bit Python on a 64-bit OS. Ended up being able to resolve the issue by ensuring the correct version of Homebrew was installed for the architecture and installing `libopm`.

# Attribution

> What did each team member do?

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Max Eichholz

-   
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Liza Mozolyuk

-   
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Zack Rahbar

-   
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Lauren Schmidt
-   Helped collect and clean weather data. 
-   Integrated features from the training approach into the classification approach, evaluating features like average delay across routes, categorical distance data, and distance transformations. 
- Developed weather trend features including change in windspeed and precipitation over the hours closest to departure. 

-   
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Ozzy Simpson

-   Helped collect and clean weather data.
-   Developed the model skeleton code/pipeline (i.e., importing our two datasets, merging them, setting up hyperparameter search, training, and evaluation).
-   Led the implementation of the classification approach, especially the version closest to @wangCalibratedExplainableFlight2026.
:::

# References

::: {#refs}
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Notes

## Data

### Weather Data

-   Source: NOAA Integrated Surface Database (ISD)
-   Timeframe: 2024-01-01 to 2025-08-27 (latest available data)
-   Location/station: DCA72405013743
-   Data format: CSV files
-   Column information: https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/noaa/isd-format-document.docx
